| Nanocarrier | Structure | Drug Loading | Example Product |
|-------------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Liposome** | Phospholipid bilayer vesicle (uni/multilamellar) | Hydrophilic core + hydrophobic bilayer | Doxil® (doxorubicin) |
| **Polymeric NP** | PLGA, PLA, chitosan matrix | Encapsulation or conjugation | Abraxane® (albumin-bound paclitaxel) |
| **Micelle** | Amphiphilic block copolymer (10–100 nm) | Hydrophobic core | Genexol-PM® (paclitaxel) |
| **Dendrimer** | Branched macromolecule (PAMAM, PPI) | Internal cavities | VivaGel® (Phase III) |
| **Gold NP** | Au core with thiol-PEG shell | Thiol-linker conjugation | AuroShell® (photothermal) |
| **Iron-oxide NP** | Fe₃O₄ core (5–20 nm) | Surface adsorption | NanoTherm® (hyperthermia) |
| **LNP (Lipid NP)** | Ionizable lipid + helper lipid + cholesterol + PEG-lipid | Ionizable head groups | Onpattro® (siRNA) |

> **[Distribution:** 6 carriers × 1 mark each + clinical example table **]**

**(b) Higuchi Derivation & Limitations (7 marks):**

Full derivation as in Section 3.1 (Steps 1–5) — **5 marks**.
Limitations — **2 marks**:

- Valid only for $C_0 \gg C_s$.
- Assumes constant geometry (no swelling/erosion).
- Ignores polymer relaxation kinetics.
- Fails at high drug loadings or near the dissolution front.
- No provision for burst release quantification in the early time window.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) Active Targeting Strategies (7 marks):**

Active targeting employs **ligand-receptor** recognition to bind overexpressed biomarkers on cancer cells. Strategies include:

1. **Folic acid conjugation** → binds FRα on ovarian, breast, lung cancers.
2. **Antibody targeting** (e.g., Trastuzumab) → binds HER2 on breast cancer.
3. **Peptide targeting** (e.g., RGD) → binds αvβ3 integrin on angiogenic endothelium.
4. **Aptamer-based** → nucleic acid ligands against PSMA, nucleolin.

**Comparison table:**

| Parameter | Passive (EPR) | Active (Ligand) |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Specificity | Low–Moderate | High |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Clinical status | Approved (Doxil) | Mostly investigational |
| Patient suitability | All solid tumors | Subset with biomarker expression |

**(b) Pharmacokinetic Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Plasma half-life:**

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{el}} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.04} = 17.33 \; \text{h}
$$

**(ii) Initial plasma concentration:**

For a 50 mg/m² dose in a 1.73 m² adult: $D = 86.5$ mg = $8.65 \times 10^{-2}$ g.

$$
C_0 = \dfrac{D}{V_d} = \dfrac{8.65 \times 10^{-2}}{4} = 2.16 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{g/L} = 21.6 \; \mu\text{g/mL}
$$

**(iii) Concentration after 12 hours:**

$$
C_{12} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_{el} \cdot t} = 21.6 \cdot e^{-0.04 \times 12} = 21.6 \cdot e^{-0.48} = 21.6 \cdot 0.619 = 13.37 \; \mu\text{g/mL}
$$

**(iv) Pharmacokinetic comment:**

- Free doxorubicin: $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / 0.35 = 1.98$ h — extremely short.
- Liposomal doxorubicin: $t_{1/2} = 17.33$ h — about **8.7× longer**.
- The liposomal formulation sustains therapeutic plasma levels far longer, allowing more drug to reach the tumor via the EPR effect while reducing peak $C_{max}$-related cardiotoxicity.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common mistake #1:** Confusing "drug-loaded" with "drug-fed" in EE/DLC formulas — this single error costs 2–3 marks. *Always re-read the denominator carefully.*
> 2. **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting the **2** in the denominator of the Higuchi equation ($Q = \sqrt{D C_s t / 2\tau}$). This is the most-tested equation — memorize it verbatim.
> 3. **Common mistake #3:** In Korsmeyer–Peppas, students often forget to specify the geometry (slab, cylinder, sphere) when interpreting $n$. KTU expects: $n = 0.45$ (slab), $n = 0.43$ (cylinder), $n = 0.45$ (sphere) for Fickian diffusion.
> 4. **Common mistake #4:** Failing to **state units** in numerical problems. Always write "$\text{kg/m}^2$" or "$\text{mg/mL}$" beside numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Common mistake #5:** Calling the EPR effect "active targeting" — it is **passive**. This conceptual error can wipe out an entire 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Nano-chemotherapy** = use of 1–100 nm carriers to deliver chemo drugs selectively.
- ✅ **EPR effect** = passive targeting via leaky tumor vasculature + poor lymphatic drainage (Maeda 1986).
- ✅ **Active targeting** = ligand-receptor binding (folate, HER2, RGD, TfR).
- ✅ **PEGylation** = stealth coating that prolongs circulation time by evading RES.
- ✅ **Higuchi equation:** $Q = \sqrt{D C_s t / (2\tau)}$ — for matrix-diffusion systems.
- ✅ **Korsmeyer–Peppas:** $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k t^n$; interpret $n$ via geometry table.
- ✅ **First-order kinetics:** $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$; half-life = $0.693/k$.
- ✅ **EE (%)** = (drug loaded / drug fed) × 100.
- ✅ **DLC (%)** = (drug loaded / carrier mass) × 100.
- ✅ **TAR** = $AUC_{\text{tumor}} / AUC_{\text{plasma}}$; clinical goal > 2.
- ✅ **T/N ratio** = $C_{\text{tumor}} / C_{\text{normal}}$; goal > 2 for selectivity.
- ✅ **Clinical benchmarks:** Doxil®, Abraxane®, Onivyde®, NanoTherm®, AuroShell®.
- ✅ **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ✅ **Stokes–Einstein:** $D_h = k_B T / (3 \pi \eta D_t)$.
- ✅ **Always state units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ **EPR is passive, NOT active** — most-confused point in KTU answer scripts.
- ✅ **Liposome = phospholipid bilayer; Micelle = amphiphile core; LNP = ionizable lipid.**
- ✅ **RGD peptide targets αvβ3 integrin on angiogenic endothelium.**
- ✅ **Trastuzumab (Herceptin) targets HER2/neu in breast cancer.**
- ✅ **MDR (Multidrug Resistance) is overcome by nanocarriers bypassing P-glycoprotein efflux.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Therapy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT632 - NANO BIOENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Therapy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Therapy (Radiotherapy / Therapeutic Radiology):** A clinical discipline of oncology that uses controlled doses of **ionizing radiation** to damage the DNA of malignant cells, leading to their loss of reproductive integrity (apoptosis or mitotic death), while preserving the surrounding healthy tissue through precise spatial, temporal, and dosimetric control.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Nano Bioengineering* (PEBBT632), radiation therapy is studied as a **therapeutic application interface** where nanomaterials (especially high-Z nanoparticles, scintillators, and radio-sensitizers) augment the radiobiological effect of conventional photon, electron, or ion beams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sniper firing at a target buried inside a fortress (the human body)**. The fortress walls are normal, healthy tissues. The sniper is the radiation beam. Conventional radiation therapy is the sniper's bullet — accurate, but it must pass through a lot of wall (healthy tissue) to hit the criminal (tumor).

Now imagine giving the criminal a **glowing paint that only shows up under the sniper's laser scope (nanoparticles)**, or worse, attaching **miniature bombs (radio-sensitizers)** to him that detonate the moment the bullet passes. That is exactly what **nanoparticle-aided radiation therapy** does. Gold nanorods, hafnium oxide nanoparticles, and quantum dots concentrate the radiation dose *specifically inside the tumor*, sparing healthy tissue.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Radiation Therapy (Bolded for Quick Recall)**
> - **Gray (Gy):** SI unit of absorbed dose. **1 Gy = 1 J/kg**.
> - **Sievert (Sv):** SI unit of equivalent dose (weighted by radiation type).
> - **Linear Energy Transfer (LET):** Energy deposited per unit path length, in **keV/µm**.
> - **Relative Biological Effectiveness (RBE):** Ratio of dose of reference radiation (250 kVp X-rays) to dose of test radiation producing same biological effect.
> - **Speed of light:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Planck's constant:** **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**

### 1.4 Classification of Radiation Therapy

```
Radiation Therapy
├── External Beam (EBRT)
│   ├── Photon beams (X-rays, γ-rays)
│   ├── Electron beams
│   └── Hadron beams (Protons, Carbon ions)
└── Internal / Brachytherapy
    ├── Temporary implants
    └── Permanent seeds (e.g., I-125, Pd-103)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Radiobiological Mechanism (The "Why")

When ionizing radiation interacts with biological tissue, it deposits energy through three primary mechanisms:

1. **Direct Effect** — Radiation directly ionizes critical biomolecules (DNA). A single photon strike can break a phosphodiester bond.
2. **Indirect Effect** — Radiation ionizes water (the dominant component of cells, ~70%), producing **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)**:
   - H₂O → H₂O⁺ + e⁻
   - e⁻ + H₂O → •OH + H⁺ + e⁻(aq)
   - The hydroxyl radical (**•OH**) is the most damaging ROS, causing **DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs)**.

3. **Bystander / Abscopal Effect** — Cells not directly hit still exhibit damage due to signaling molecules and immune modulation.

### 2.2 The 4 D's of Radiobiology (Key Conceptual Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 D's of Cell Death Following Radiation:**
> - **Damage** — Initial physical/chemical insult to DNA.
> - **Disrepair** — Mis-repaired or unrepaired DNA lesions.
> - **Doubling (mitotic failure)** — Cell attempts division, fails.
> - **Death** — Apoptosis, necrosis, or senescence.

### 2.3 Linear-Quadratic (LQ) Model — The Heart of Modern Dose Calculation

The **LQ Model** is the universally accepted mathematical description of cell survival after radiation exposure.

$$S = e^{-\alpha D - \beta D^2}$$

Where:
- $S$ = surviving fraction of cells
- $D$ = radiation dose (Gy)
- $\alpha$ = linear component (single-hit, irreparable damage)
- $\beta$ = quadratic component (two-hit, reparable damage)
- $\alpha/\beta$ ratio = tissue-specific radio-sensitivity (low for late-responding tissues like spinal cord, high for early-responding tissues like intestinal mucosa)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $D = \frac{E}{m}$ | Absorbed dose definition | Gy (J/kg) |
| 2 | $S = e^{-\alpha D - \beta D^2}$ | Cell survival (LQ Model) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{RE} = \frac{D_{\text{test}}}{D_{250\,\text{kVp}}}$ | Relative Effectiveness | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $D_{eq} = D \left(1 + \frac{d}{(\alpha/\beta)}\right)$ | Equivalent dose for fractionation | Gy |
| 5 | $\text{TER} = \frac{\text{LET}_{\text{particle}}}{\text{LET}_{250\,\text{kVp X}}}$ | Therapeutic Enhancement Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $H = D \times W_R$ | Equivalent dose (Sievert) | Sv |
| 7 | $N(x) = N_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Photon attenuation in tissue | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{OER} = \frac{D_{\text{hypoxic}}}{D_{\text{oxic}}}$ | Oxygen Enhancement Ratio | ~2.5–3 for photons |
| 9 | $\text{SPR} = \frac{\text{Dose}_{\text{with NP}}}{\text{Dose}_{\text{without NP}}}$ | Sensitization Power Ratio (Nanotech) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Precaution for Table Cells:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

### 2.5 Why Nanoparticles? — The Engineering Rationale

High-Z (high atomic number) nanoparticles such as **Gold (Z=79)**, **Hafnium oxide (HfO₂, Z=72)**, **Gadolinium (Z=64)**, and **Bismuth (Z=83)** are excellent radiation sensitizers because:

- The **mass attenuation coefficient ($\mu/\rho$)** scales roughly as **$Z^3$ to $Z^4$** in the keV range.
- When a nanoparticle is endocytosed by tumor cells, it locally amplifies photoelectric absorption, generating **Auger electrons** and **secondary electrons** that dump energy right at the DNA.
- This gives a **dose enhancement factor (DEF)** of **1.5× to 4×** in vitro.

### 2.6 Real-World Clinical Engineering Utility

- **Cancer hospitals (e.g., TMH Mumbai, AIIMS):** 60% of cancer patients receive radiotherapy.
- **Hadron therapy centers:** Spread across Europe, Japan, and now India (Apollo Hospitals, Tata Medical Center).
- **Industrial spin-offs:** Carbon-ion therapy, FLASH radiotherapy (>40 Gy/s) are emerging frontiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Linear-Quadratic Model from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** Cell killing by radiation can be modeled as two independent Poisson processes: (a) single lethal event, (b) accumulation of two sublethal events producing a lethal combination.

**Step 1 — Define the probability of survival for a single lethal hit:**

Let $\lambda$ be the average number of lethal lesions per cell at dose $D$. Since lesions follow Poisson statistics, the probability of zero lethal hits (i.e., survival) is:

$$S_1 = e^{-\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\lambda$ to dose:**

For the linear (single-hit) component, lesions scale directly with dose, giving $\lambda_1 = \alpha D$. Therefore:

$$S_1 = e^{-\alpha D}$$

**Step 3 — Include the quadratic component:**

For two-hit killing, the rate of lethal events depends on the **square** of sublethal lesion density. The sublethal lesion count at dose $D$ is proportional to $D$, so the two-hit killing rate scales as $D^2$, contributing a term $\beta D^2$ to the exponent.

$$S_2 = e^{-\beta D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine both processes (independent events):**

Since survival requires escaping *both* killing mechanisms, and the two processes are statistically independent:

$$S = S_1 \cdot S_2$$

Substituting:

$$S = e^{-\alpha D} \cdot e^{-\beta D^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the exponent (rule of exponents):**

Using the identity $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$:

$$\boxed{S = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^2)}}$$

This is the celebrated **Linear-Quadratic (LQ) model** of cell survival.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dose Equivalence for Fractionated Treatment

**Starting Premise:** Clinicians split a total dose $D_T$ into $n$ equal fractions of size $d = D_T/n$. We want an "equivalent" total dose that produces the same biological effect for a different fractionation schedule.

**Step 1 — Survival after $n$ fractions of size $d$:**

$$S = \left(e^{-\alpha d - \beta d^2}\right)^n = e^{-n(\alpha d + \beta d^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Survival for a single dose $D$:**

$$S = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^2)}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two exponents:**

$$n(\alpha d + \beta d^2) = \alpha D_{eq} + \beta D_{eq}^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D_{eq}$ using the standard approximation** (assume $\alpha D_{eq}$ dominates for small changes):

Factor the right-hand side as $\alpha D_{eq} \left(1 + \dfrac{\beta D_{eq}}{\alpha}\right)$. The fractionation effect is then:

$$D_{eq} = D_T \cdot \frac{d + (\alpha/\beta)}{(\alpha/\beta) + (\alpha/\beta)} \cdot \frac{1}{...}$$

The accepted clinical formula (from Thames & Hendry) is:

$$\boxed{D_{eq} = D_T \left(1 + \frac{d}{(\alpha/\beta)}\right)}$$

This formula explains **why hyperfractionation** (smaller doses, more fractions) spares late-responding normal tissues (which have low $\alpha/\beta$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Photon Attenuation in Tissue (Beer-Lambert Analog)

**Step 1 — Differential equation for photon flux:**

The number of photons removed per unit path length is proportional to the local flux $\Phi(x)$:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dx} = -\mu \Phi(x)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{d\Phi}{\Phi} = -\mu \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $x$:**

$$\int_{\Phi_0}^{\Phi(x)} \frac{d\Phi}{\Phi} = -\mu \int_0^x dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Phi(x)}{\Phi_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**

$$\boxed{\Phi(x) = \Phi_0 \, e^{-\mu x}}$$

This is the **exponential attenuation law** governing how a photon beam loses intensity as it penetrates tissue. For high-Z nanoparticle-loaded tumors, the local $\mu$ increases sharply via the photoelectric effect, enhancing dose deposition.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Photoelectric Mass Attenuation Coefficient Dependence on Z

**Step 1 — Start with the Kramers–Heitler photoelectric cross-section (non-relativistic form):**

The atomic cross-section for the photoelectric effect in the K-shell is:

$$\sigma_{pe} \propto \frac{Z^{n}}{E^3}$$

where $n$ is between 4 and 5, and $E$ is photon energy.

**Step 2 — Convert to mass attenuation coefficient:**

The mass attenuation coefficient is $\mu/\rho = (N_A/A) \cdot \sigma_{pe}$, where $N_A$ is Avogadro's number and $A$ is atomic mass.

For biological tissues, $A \approx 2Z$ (light elements), so:

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho} \propto \frac{Z^5}{E^{3.5}}$$

(in the diagnostic keV energy range, $n \approx 4$–$5$).

**Step 3 — Conclusion for nanoparticles:**

If we replace a soft tissue background ($Z_{\text{eff}} \approx 7.4$) with a gold nanoparticle ($Z = 79$), the ratio of mass attenuation coefficients is:

$$\frac{\mu_{Au}}{\mu_{\text{tissue}}} = \left(\frac{79}{7.4}\right)^{4.5} \approx 6.6 \times 10^4$$

This enormous factor is the **physical foundation** of nanoparticle-enhanced radiotherapy. The number is dramatic but realistic in the 20–100 keV X-ray range used in brachytherapy.

> [!NOTE]
> The above ratio is illustrative. In clinical MV beams (e.g., 6 MV Linac), Compton scattering dominates and the $Z$ dependence softens to $Z^1$–$Z^2$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Radiation Modalities

| Modality | Source | Typical Energy | LET (keV/µm) | RBE | Best Clinical Use |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------------|-----|------------------|
| **60Co γ-rays** | Cobalt-60 | 1.17 & 1.33 MeV | ~0.2 | 1.0 | Teletherapy (legacy) |
| **6 MV X-rays** | Linac | 6 MeV peak | ~0.2 | 1.0 | EBRT, deep tumors |
| **Electrons** | Linac | 6–20 MeV | ~0.2 | 1.0 | Superficial tumors |
| **250 kVp X-rays** | X-ray tube | 250 keV | ~2.0 | 1.0 (reference) | Reference standard |
| **Protons** | Cyclotron/Synchrotron | 70–250 MeV | ~1–5 (entrance), 100 (Bragg peak) | 1.1 | Pediatric, ocular, skull base |
| **Carbon ions** | Synchrotron | 100–400 MeV/u | 50–200 (Bragg peak) | 2–5 | Radio-resistant tumors |
| **Neutrons** | Reactor/Accelerator | 14 MeV (d-T) | 10–100 | 3–5 | Salivary gland tumors |
| **Brachytherapy seeds** | Ir-192, I-125 | 20–400 keV | ~0.5 | 1.0 | Prostate, breast, cervix |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Workflow of a Modern Radiotherapy Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Diagnosis] --> B[CT MRI PET Simulation]
    B --> C[Tumor Delineation by Radiation Oncologist]
    C --> D[Treatment Planning System TPS]
    D --> E[Inverse Optimization with Dose Constraints]
    E --> F{Plan Approval}
    F -->|Reject| D
    F -->|Approve| G[Quality Assurance QA]
    G --> H[Patient Positioning on Couch]
    H --> I[Image Guided Radiotherapy IGRT]
    I --> J[Linear Accelerator LINAC Delivery]
    J --> K[Real time Dose Monitoring EPID]
    K --> L[Follow up and Dosimetric Review]
    L --> M[Adaptive Re planning if Needed]
    M --> N[End of Treatment]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Nanoparticle-Augmented Radio-Sensitization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tumor Cell] --> B[Endocytosis of Au Nanoparticles]
    B --> C[Intracellular Accumulation]
    C --> D[External X ray Beam Irradiation]
    D --> E[Photoelectric Absorption in Au Atoms]
    E --> F[Emission of Auger Electrons]
    E --> G[Emission of Photoelectrons]
    F --> H[Localized DNA Double Strand Breaks]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Cell Cycle Arrest at G2 M Checkpoint]
    I --> J[Apoptosis or Mitotic Catastrophe]
    J --> K[Tumor Volume Reduction]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Linear Accelerator Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage Pulse Transformer] --> B[Electron Gun Injector]
    B --> C[Klystron or Magnetron Microwave Source]
    C --> D[Waveguide]
    D --> E[Accelerating Structure Travelling Wave]
    E --> F[Bending Magnet]
    F --> G[Treatment Head Assembly]
    G --> H[Primary Collimator]
    H --> I[Secondary Jaws]
    I --> J[Multileaf Collimator MLC]
    J --> K[Beam Exit Window]
    K --> L[Patient Surface]
    L --> M[EPID Electronic Portal Imaging Device]
    M --> N[Dose Verification Feedback Loop]
    N --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Comparison of External Beam vs Brachytherapy Dose Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EBRT[External Beam Radiotherapy]
        EA[Source far from body 80 to 100 cm] --> EB[Beam enters through normal tissue]
        EB --> EC[Exits through normal tissue on opposite side]
        EC --> ED[High dose volume includes tumor and surrounding tissue]
    end
    subgraph BT[Brachytherapy]
        BA[Source placed inside or next to tumor] --> BB[Inverse square law falloff]
        BB --> BC[Concentrates dose in target]
        BC --> BD[Surrounding tissue receives rapidly decreasing dose]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above replace physical drawings (e.g., isodose contour plots) with **block-level functional architecture** flows, as required by the protocol for complex physical layouts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBBT632]**
*Define Linear Energy Transfer (LET) and Relative Biological Effectiveness (RBE). Why are high-LET radiations preferred for treating hypoxic tumors?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **LET** is the energy transferred per unit path length by ionizing radiation, expressed in **keV/µm**. **(1 mark)**
- **RBE** is the ratio of the dose of a reference radiation (250 kVp X-rays) to the dose of the test radiation required to produce the same biological effect. **(1 mark)**
- High-LET radiations (e.g., α-particles, carbon ions) deposit dense ionization tracks along their path, producing **complex, clustered DNA damage** that is harder for cellular repair mechanisms to fix. Hypoxic tumor cells, which are typically radio-resistant under low-LET photons (OER ≈ 2.5–3), show a much **reduced OER** (~1.0) under high-LET beams. Hence, high-LET radiation is preferred for radio-resistant hypoxic tumors. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT632]**
*What is the Oxygen Enhancement Ratio (OER)? State its typical value for 250 kVp X-rays and comment on its clinical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **OER** is defined as the ratio of radiation dose required to produce the same biological effect under hypoxic conditions to that under fully oxygenated conditions. **(1 mark)**
- Typical OER for **250 kVp X-rays ≈ 2.5–3.0**, meaning hypoxic cells need 2.5–3 times more dose to be killed. **(1 mark)**
- **Clinical significance:** Solid tumors contain hypoxic regions (10–20% of cells) that survive conventional photon therapy and cause tumor recurrence. Strategies like **hypoxic cell radiosensitizers**, **high-LET radiation**, and **oxygen-mimetic drugs** are used to overcome this limitation. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Path A

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBBT632, Module 3]**

*(a)* Explain in detail the **principle and working of a 6 MV Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** used in external beam radiotherapy. Include a labeled block diagram of the treatment head. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Derive the **Linear-Quadratic (LQ) model of cell survival** starting from the Poisson distribution. Discuss how the $\alpha/\beta$ ratio influences fractionation schedules. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Linear Accelerator — Principle and Working (7 marks)**

1. **Basic principle (1 mark):** A LINAC accelerates electrons to near-light speeds using microwave electromagnetic fields (frequency ≈ 3 GHz from a magnetron or klystron). The high-energy electron beam can be used directly (electron therapy) or struck into a high-Z target to produce **X-rays (Bremsstrahlung)**.

2. **Major subsystems (3 marks):**
   - **Modulator & Pulse Forming Network:** Generates high-voltage pulses (~5–15 kV) for the electron gun and klystron.
   - **Microwave source (Klystron/Magnetron):** Produces 3 GHz microwaves; klystrons are used for higher energies and dual-energy modes.
   - **Waveguide & Accelerating structure:** Travelling-wave or standing-wave copper cavity where electrons gain energy (~2 MeV/m).
   - **Bending magnet (270° achromatic):** Redirects the horizontal beam to vertical, filtering low-energy electrons.
   - **Treatment head components:** Primary collimator → Secondary jaws (X, Y) → Multileaf Collimator (MLC, 120 leaves) → Optional wedges / dynamic MLC for IMRT.

3. **Working steps (2 marks):**
   - Electron gun injects electrons (~50 keV) into the waveguide.
   - Microwaves from klystron accelerate them in bunches through the drift tube.
   - At exit, electrons have kinetic energy 6 MeV (or 15/18 MeV for higher modes).
   - For photon mode, electrons strike a tungsten target → **Bremsstrahlung X-ray spectrum** produced.
   - Beam exits through a flattening filter, is collimated, and reaches the patient.
   - **EPID (Electronic Portal Imaging Device)** captures exit dose for QA.

4. **Dosimetric features (1 mark):** Flattened beam with maximum dose at ~1.5 cm depth in water ($d_{max}$), with 80% dose at 10 cm and 50% at ~18 cm (PDD curves).

---

**(b) Derivation of the LQ Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Let $S(D)$ be the surviving fraction. Consider a single lethal event. The number of lesions per cell is Poisson-distributed with mean $\lambda$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For a single-hit (linear) event, $\lambda_1 = \alpha D$. The probability of *zero* lethal events (survival) is:

$$S_1 = e^{-\alpha D}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For two-hit killing, the rate depends on the *square* of sublethal lesion density. Sublethal events scale as $D$, so lethal two-hit events scale as $D^2$:

$$S_2 = e^{-\beta D^2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Survival requires escaping both mechanisms. Assuming independence:

$$S(D) = S_1 \cdot S_2 = e^{-\alpha D} \cdot e^{-\beta D^2} = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^2)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Clinical utility of $\alpha/\beta$ (2 marks):**
- High $\alpha/\beta$ (≥ 10 Gy): early-responding tissues (gut, mucosa, tumors) — benefit from large fractions.
- Low $\alpha/\beta$ (≤ 3 Gy): late-responding tissues (spinal cord, kidney, prostate) — strongly spared by small fractions.
- The formula $D_{eq} = D_T(1 + d/(\alpha/\beta))$ quantifies this. For prostate cancer ($\alpha/\beta \approx 1.5$ Gy), **hypofractionation** (large dose per fraction) gives superior tumor control with manageable toxicity — this is the basis of **SBRT (Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Path B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT632, Module 3]**

*(a)* Describe the **principle of brachytherapy**. Compare temporary and permanent implants with suitable clinical examples. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **role of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) in radiation dose enhancement**, with quantitative justification using the photoelectric effect. Show how the dose enhancement factor (DEF) is calculated. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Brachytherapy — Principle and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):** Brachytherapy (Greek *brachys* = short) places a **sealed radioactive source inside or immediately adjacent to the tumor volume**, exploiting the **inverse square law** to deliver a high dose to the target while sparing surrounding normal tissue. The dose falls off as $1/r^2$, providing rapid falloff.

**Temporary implants (2 marks):**
- **High Dose Rate (HDR):** Ir-192 source (380 keV), 10 Gy/min, dwell times of seconds to minutes. Afterloading with plastic catheters; source is removed after each session.
- **Clinical examples:** Carcinoma cervix (Manchester/ICRU applicators), endobronchial tumors, breast (MammoSite balloon).
- Patient is radioactive only during the session.

**Permanent implants (2 marks):**
- **Low Dose Rate (LDR):** I-125 (27–35 keV, $t_{1/2}$ = 60 days) or Pd-103 seeds (20–23 keV, $t_{1/2}$ = 17 days).
- **Clinical example:** Prostate cancer (transperineal template-guided seed implantation, TG-43 dose formalism).
- Seeds remain permanently; patient emits low radiation for several months (precautions for pregnant women and children).

**Comparison summary (1 mark):** HDR allows dose sculpting and is outpatient-friendly; LDR gives continuous low-dose radiobiological effect (high $\alpha/\beta$ advantage for slow-growing tumors) but requires radiation safety precautions.

---

**(b) Gold Nanoparticles in Radiation Dose Enhancement (7 marks)**

**Mechanism (2 marks):** When AuNPs (Z=79) accumulate in tumor cells via the **EPR effect** (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) and active targeting (e.g., folic acid conjugation), they act as **in-situ radiation amplifiers**. Under keV X-ray irradiation (e.g., 50–100 kVp brachytherapy), the photoelectric effect dominates, and the high Z of gold causes intense local absorption.

**Quantitative justification (3 marks):** The mass attenuation coefficient scales as:

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho} \propto \frac{Z^n}{E^3}, \quad n \approx 4\text{–}5 \text{ (in keV range)}$$

For a tumor loaded with 1% by mass AuNPs vs. soft tissue ($Z_{\text{eff}} \approx 7.4$):

$$\text{DEF}_{\text{local}} = \frac{Z_{Au}^{4.5}}{Z_{\text{tissue}}^{4.5}} = \left(\frac{79}{7.4}\right)^{4.5} \approx 6.6 \times 10^4$$

In practice, dilution by cellular water and limited intracellular Au concentrations give a realistic **DEF of 1.5–3** (clinically meaningful).

**Calculating DEF (1 mark):**

$$\text{DEF} = \frac{D_{\text{tumor, with AuNP}}}{D_{\text{tumor, no AuNP}}}$$

Measured experimentally using clonogenic assays or Monte Carlo (GEANT4) simulations. AuNPs of 1.9 nm diameter conjugated to anti-EGFR antibodies have shown DEF ≈ 2.7 in head & neck cancer cell lines (Hainfeld et al., 2004).

**Engineering challenges (1 mark):** Toxicity (liver/kidney accumulation), clearance half-life, scale-up synthesis, regulatory pathway (FDA/ICMR approval).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not confuse absorbed dose (Gy) with equivalent dose (Sv).** Gy measures energy deposited; Sv accounts for radiation type via $W_R$.
> - **LQ model $\alpha/\beta$ is in Gy, not dimensionless.** Markers often deduct marks for unit errors.
> - **In OER definition**, write the ratio as $D_{\text{hypoxic}} / D_{\text{oxic}}$, *not* the inverse. Direction matters.
> - **For gold nanoparticles**, do not claim the theoretical $10^4$ enhancement is clinically achievable; mention the realistic 1.5–3× figure. Examiners penalize unrealistic extrapolation.
> - **Brachytherapy** is *not* "internal radiation therapy" in the unsealed radiopharmaceutical sense (e.g., I-131 for thyroid). It uses *sealed* sources — distinguish clearly.
> - **LINAC energy**: 6 MV refers to the *photon* nominal energy, not the electron energy. The peak electron energy in the waveguide is ~6 MeV.
> - Always show units in formula substitution. Marks are often lost on missing Gy, keV/µm, or % symbols.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Radiation Therapy (KTU 2024 Module 3)**
> - ✅ **Radiation therapy = controlled ionization** to kill cancer cells while sparing normal tissue.
> - ✅ **Direct effect** (DNA ionization) + **Indirect effect** (•OH radicals from water radiolysis).
> - ✅ **4 D's of radiobiology:** Damage → Disrepair → Doubling → Death.
> - ✅ **Absorbed dose** $D$ in **Gy**; **Equivalent dose** $H$ in **Sv** ($H = D \times W_R$).
> - ✅ **LQ model** $S = e^{-(\alpha D + \beta D^2)}$ — memorize and be ready to derive.
> - ✅ **$\alpha/\beta$ ratio** < 3 Gy (late tissues) vs. > 10 Gy (early tissues/tumors).
> - ✅ **OER** ≈ 2.5–3 for photons, ≈ 1 for high-LET — critical for radio-resistant tumors.
> - ✅ **LET** for photons ≈ 0.2 keV/µm; for carbon ions ≈ 100 keV/µm at Bragg peak.
> - ✅ **Brachytherapy** = sealed sources; **inverse square law** gives steep dose falloff.
> - ✅ **HDR (Ir-192)** vs **LDR (I-125, Pd-103)** brachytherapy — know the radionuclide energies and half-lives.
> - ✅ **LINAC** = 3 GHz microwave acceleration (klystron/magnetron) → Bremsstrahlung target.
> - ✅ **Nanoparticle enhancement:** $\mu/\rho \propto Z^4$–$Z^5$ in keV range; clinical DEF = 1.5–3.
> - ✅ **Au, Hf, Gd, Bi** are key high-Z nanoparticle candidates; **HfO₂ (NBTXR3)** is in Phase III trials.
> - ✅ **EPR effect** = passive tumor targeting; active targeting uses antibodies (cetuximab) or folic acid.
> - ✅ **FLASH radiotherapy** (>40 Gy/s) shows normal tissue sparing — emerging frontier.
> - ✅ **Hadron therapy (protons/carbon)** exploits the **Bragg peak** for distal dose conformity.
> - ✅ **Common exam pitfalls:** unit errors, confusing Gy vs Sv, missing the "sealed vs unsealed" distinction in brachytherapy.
> - ✅ **Clinical terms to know:** PDD (Percentage Depth Dose), IMRT, IGRT, SBRT, VMAT, EPID, MLC, MLC, MLC, OAR (Organs at Risk), TCP (Tumor Control Probability), NTCP (Normal Tissue Complication Probability).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — RADIATION THERAPY (PEBBT632 / KTU 2024 SCHEME)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Medicine Therapy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT632 - NANO BIOENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Nuclear Medicine Therapy # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Nuclear Medicine Therapy (NMT)**, also termed **Targeted Radionuclide Therapy (TRT)** or **Molecular Radiotherapy (MRT)**, is a specialized branch of clinical oncology and molecular medicine that uses **unsealed radioactive sources (radiopharmaceuticals)** administered systemically or locoregionally to deliver cytotoxic ionizing radiation **selectively to diseased cells** (tumour, hyperfunctioning tissue, or pathological lesions) while sparing surrounding healthy tissue. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3, Nano Bioengineering), Nuclear Medicine Therapy represents the convergence of three disciplines: 1. **Radiation Physics** (radioactive decay, dosimetry) 2. **Radiopharmaceutical Chemistry** (radionuclide + targeting vector) 3. **Molecular Biology / Nanomedicine** (receptors, antibodies, nanoparticles as carriers) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** NMT is distinguished from *external beam radiotherapy* (EBRT) because the radiation source is **internal** — physically incorporated into the patient's body through a targeting biomolecule, peptide, antibody, or nanoparticle carrier. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bomb" of Medicine Imagine you are a surgeon tasked with destroying only the *guerrilla fighters* hiding inside a vast city without harming any civilians. You cannot bomb the entire city (that would be conventional chemotherapy). Instead, you design a **guided missile** that: - Recognizes a unique marker (antigen / receptor) present only on the enemy. - Carries an explosive payload (radionuclide emitting α, β⁻, or Auger electrons). - Steers itself to the target tissue (receptor-mediated endocytosis / active transport). **In NMT, the "missile" is the radiopharmaceutical, and the "warhead" is the radionuclide.** | Component | Biological Analogy | Engineering Reality | |-----------|-------------------|---------------------| | Targeting Vector | Missile guidance system | Antibody, peptide, ligand, nanoparticle | | Radionuclide | Explosive warhead | I-131, Lu-177, Y-90, Ra-223, Ac-225 | | Chelator | Coupling ring | DTPA, DOTA, NOTA | | Disease marker | Enemy signature | PSMA, somatostatin receptor, Tg, CD20 | --- ## 1.3 Classification of Therapeutic Radionuclides by Emission Type > [!NOTE] > The choice of emission determines **Linear Energy Transfer (LET)**, tissue range, and biological effectiveness — a fundamental KTU high-yield concept. | Emission Type | Radionuclide Examples | LET (keV/μm) | Tissue Range | Best Use | |---------------|----------------------|---------------|--------------|----------| | **β⁻ (Beta minus)** | I-131, Lu-177, Y-90, Sm-153, Re-188 | 0.2 – 1.0 | 0.5 – 12 mm | Bulky tumours, micrometastases | | **α (Alpha)** | Ra-223, Ac-225, Bi-213, At-211 | 50 – 230 | 40 – 100 μm | Single cells, micro-metastases | | **Auger electrons** | I-125, In-111, Tc-99m | 4 – 26 | 2 – 500 nm | DNA-adjacent / nuclear targets | The **Standard Decay Constant** for any radionuclide: $$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$ where the **Half-life (T₁/₂)** of a therapeutic radionuclide typically lies between **6 hours and 14 days** to match the *biological half-life* of the carrier molecule. --- ## 1.4 Engineering Metrics & Standard Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > These are the standard physical and dosimetric constants used throughout KTU Nano Bioengineering problems: - **Avogadro's Number:** **N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** - **Mean Life:** **τ = 1/λ** - **Activity unit:** **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** - **Absorbed dose unit:** **1 Gy = 1 J/kg** - **Equivalent dose unit:** **1 Sv = 1 J/kg (weighted)** - **Quality factor (β):** W_R = **1** - **Quality factor (α):** W_R = **20** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Six-Step Therapeutic Cascade Nuclear Medicine Therapy follows a precise **biological-operational chain**. Understanding each step is mandatory for KTU exam answers. ### Step 1: Radiopharmaceutical Synthesis A **radionuclide** is chemically bound (via a **bifunctional chelator** such as DOTA or DTPA) to a **targeting biomolecule** (antibody, peptide, small molecule, or nanoparticle). ### Step 2: Administration Routes include: - **Intravenous (i.v.)** — most common (e.g., Lu-177-DOTATATE) - **Oral** — e.g., Na¹³¹I capsules for thyroid - **Intra-arterial / intra-cavitary** — e.g., Y-90 microspheres for hepatic tumours - **Intratumoural / intravesical** — for localized cancers ### Step 3: Biodistribution & Targeting The vector binds to a disease-specific receptor: - **I-131** → Sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) on thyroid cells - **Lu-177-PSMA-617** → Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen - **Lu-177-DOTATATE** → Somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2) - **Ra-223 dichloride** → Mimics Ca²⁺, incorporates into bone hydroxyapatite ### Step 4: Internalization (where applicable) Receptor-mediated endocytosis brings the radionuclide into the cytoplasm or nucleus — critical for **Auger emitters** which require proximity to DNA. ### Step 5: Radioactive Decay & Local Energy Deposition The radionuclide emits ionizing radiation. Damage is exerted primarily through: - **DNA double-strand breaks** (DSBs) - **Reactive oxygen species (ROS)** generation - **Bystander effect** — neighbouring cells receive damage signals ### Step 6: Clearance & Radioprotection Unbound radiopharmaceutical is excreted renally (peptides, small molecules) or hepatobiliary (antibodies, nanoparticles). The **biologically effective half-life** is given by: $$T_{eff} = \frac{T_{bio} \times T_{phys}}{T_{bio} + T_{phys}}$$ where **T_bio** is the biological half-life and **T_phys** is the physical half-life of the radionuclide. --- ## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters - **Why chelate?** Free radionuclides accumulate non-specifically in bone marrow, kidney, liver → toxicity. The chelator *anchors* the metal until decay. - **Why match half-life?** If T_phys ≫ T_bio, the radionuclide decays after clearance → wasted dose. If T_phys ≪ T_bio, decay happens before targeting → no tumour dose. - **Why α vs β?** α particles have **high LET (50–230 keV/μm)** and short range → ideal for **micrometastases & circulating tumour cells**. β⁻ has **low LET but longer range** → ideal for **bulk tumours**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Bank** — memorize all rows. | # | Quantity / Law | Equation | Units | Where Used | |---|---------------|----------|-------|-----------| | 1 | Radioactive decay law | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | atoms | Decay calculations | | 2 | Activity | $A = \lambda N$ | Bq (s⁻¹) | Source strength | | 3 | Decay constant | $\lambda = \ln(2)/T_{1/2}$ | s⁻¹ | Half-life problems | | 4 | Activity vs. time | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Bq | Dose over time | | 5 | Cumulative activity | $\tilde{A} = \int_0^\infty A(t)\, dt = A_0/\lambda$ | Bq·s | MIRD dosimetry | | 6 | MIRD absorbed dose | $D_{target} = \tilde{A}_{source} \times S$ | Gy | Organ dosimetry | | 7 | Absorbed dose | $D = E_{dep}/m$ | Gy | Direct definition | | 8 | Dose rate (point source) | $\dot{D} = A \cdot \Delta \cdot \Phi / m$ | Gy/s | Local dosimetry | | 9 | Equivalent dose | $H = D \times W_R$ | Sv | Radiation weighting | | 10 | Effective dose | $E = \sum_T H_T \times W_T$ | Sv | Whole-body risk | | 11 | Effective half-life | $1/T_{eff} = 1/T_{bio} + 1/T_{phys}$ | time | Clearance modelling | | 12 | Mean (average) life | $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/\ln 2$ | time | Total decay stats | | 13 | Mass-energy conversion | $E = mc^2$ (c = 3×10⁸ m/s) | J | Energy per decay | | 14 | Specific activity | $a_s = A/m$ | Bq/g | Radiopharmaceutical purity | | 15 | Number of disintegrations | $\Delta N = N_0(1 - e^{-\lambda t})$ | atoms | Decay over interval | | 16 | LET | $LET = -dE/dx$ | keV/μm | Radiobiological effectiveness | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU answer sheets, **always quote numerical answers with units and significant figures**. Examiners deduct marks for unitless answers. --- ## 2.4 Engineering & Clinical Real-World Utility - **Oncology:** Lu-177-PSMA-617 (Pluvicto™) for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer; Lu-177-DOTATATE (Lutathera™) for GEP-NETs. - **Thyroid disorders:** I-131 NaI for Graves' disease, hyperthyroidism, and differentiated thyroid carcinoma. - **Bone metastases:** Ra-223 dichloride (Xofigo™) — first α-emitter approved for bone pain palliation. - **Hepatocellular carcinoma / Liver metastases:** Y-90 microspheres (SIR-Spheres®, TheraSphere®). - **Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma:** I-131 Tositumomab (Bexxar), Y-90 Ibritumomab tiuxetan (Zevalin) — *radioimmunotherapy*. - **Nanomedicine frontier:** Nano-carriers (liposomes, polymeric NPs, gold NPs, dendrimers) loaded with Re-188, Lu-177, or Ac-225 — direct intersection with KTU Nano Bioengineering. --- ## 2.5 Radiobiological Effectiveness & Therapeutic Index The **Therapeutic Index (TI)** of NMT is: $$TI = \frac{Tumour\ Absorbed\ Dose}{Critical\ Organ\ Absorbed\ Dose}$$ The goal of any radiopharmaceutical design is to **maximize TI**. Strategies include: - **Tumour-specific antigens** (high uptake ratio) - **Pretargeting** strategies (reduce circulating activity) - **Nano-encapsulation** (enhanced permeability and retention — EPR effect) # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Radioactive Decay Law Starting from the empirical observation that the rate of disintegration is **proportional to the number of unstable nuclei N(t)** present: $$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$ **Step 1:** Separate variables. $$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Integrate from time 0 to t, with initial population $N_0$. $$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals. $$\left[\ln N\right]_{N_0}^{N(t)} = -\lambda t$$ **Step 4:** Apply logarithm properties. $$\ln N(t) - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t$$ **Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides. $$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 6:** Because Activity $A = \lambda N$, multiply both sides by $\lambda$ to obtain the *activity decay* form. $$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 7:** Define the half-life as the time when $A(t) = A_0/2$. $$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$ **Step 8:** Solve for $\lambda$. $$e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda T_{1/2} = \ln 2$$ **Step 9:** Final expression. $$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \approx \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cumulative Activity (MIRD Foundation) In MIRD (Medical Internal Radiation Dose) formalism, the **cumulative activity** $\tilde{A}$ is the *time-integrated activity* — total number of nuclear transformations within the source organ: **Step 1:** Start with instantaneous activity. $$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 2:** Integrate from $t = 0$ to $\infty$ to obtain total disintegrations. $$\tilde{A} = \int_0^\infty A(t)\, dt = \int_0^\infty A_0 e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Pull the constant out. $$\tilde{A} = A_0 \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the integral. $$\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[\frac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute. $$\boxed{\tilde{A} = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{A_0 \, T_{1/2}}{\ln 2} = A_0 \, \tau}$$ > [!NOTE] > The cumulative activity equals the initial activity multiplied by the mean life $\tau$. Numerically, $\tilde{A} \approx 1.4427 \times A_0 \times T_{1/2}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Absorbed Dose (MIRD Equation) The absorbed dose to a target organ from a source organ is: $$D_{target} = \tilde{A}_{source} \times S(target \leftarrow source)$$ where **S** is the **S-value** (Gy/Bq·s) which depends on: - Radionuclide emission spectrum - Source organ mass - Target organ mass - Geometry (cross-organ geometry) **Step 1:** Energy deposited per unit mass is energy emitted × fraction absorbed. $$\frac{dE}{dm}$$ **Step 2:** Number of disintegrations is $\tilde{A}$. $$\text{Total energy emitted} = \tilde{A} \times \sum_i Y_i \, E_i$$ where $Y_i$ is the yield (fraction of decays producing emission $i$) and $E_i$ is its energy. **Step 3:** Fraction absorbed in target is $\phi_i$ (Absorbed Fraction). $$\text{Energy to target} = \tilde{A} \times \sum_i Y_i \, E_i \, \phi_i$$ **Step 4:** Absorbed dose = energy/mass. $$D = \frac{\tilde{A}}{m_{target}} \sum_i Y_i \, E_i \, \phi_i$$ **Step 5:** Group the geometry-dependent terms into S. $$\boxed{D_{target} = \tilde{A}_{source} \times S(target \leftarrow source)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Decay Calculation > **Problem (KTU-Style):** A patient is administered **200 MBq of Lu-177-DOTATATE**. The physical half-life of Lu-177 is **6.64 days**. Calculate the activity remaining after **14 days**. **Given:** $$A_0 = 200 \text{ MBq}, \quad T_{1/2} = 6.64 \text{ days}, \quad t = 14 \text{ days}$$ **Step 1:** Compute decay constant. $$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{6.64 \text{ days}} = 0.1044 \text{ day}^{-1}$$ **Step 2:** Convert to seconds if needed (for SI). $$\lambda = \frac{0.1044}{86400} = 1.208 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3:** Apply decay equation. $$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ $$A(14) = 200 \times e^{-(0.1044)(14)}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate exponent. $$-0.1044 \times 14 = -1.4616$$ **Step 5:** Compute $e^{-1.4616}$. $$e^{-1.4616} = 0.2321$$ **Step 6:** Final activity. $$A(14) = 200 \times 0.2321 = 46.4 \text{ MBq}$$ **Result:** After 14 days, **46.4 MBq** of Lu-177 remains — only **23.2%** of the injected activity. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Equivalent & Effective Dose > **Problem:** An organ receives absorbed dose of **2.5 Gy** from a mixed β⁻ + α radiation field. If 80% of dose is from β⁻ and 20% from α, compute (a) equivalent dose (b) effective dose if tissue weighting factor $W_T = 0.12$. **Step 1:** Decompose absorbed dose. $$D_\beta = 0.80 \times 2.5 = 2.0 \text{ Gy}$$ $$D_\alpha = 0.20 \times 2.5 = 0.5 \text{ Gy}$$ **Step 2:** Apply radiation weighting factors. - $W_{R,\beta} = 1$ - $W_{R,\alpha} = 20$ **Step 3:** Equivalent dose from each component. $$H_\beta = 2.0 \times 1 = 2.0 \text{ Sv}$$ $$H_\alpha = 0.5 \times 20 = 10.0 \text{ Sv}$$ **Step 4:** Total equivalent dose. $$H_T = 2.0 + 10.0 = 12.0 \text{ Sv}$$ **Step 5:** Effective dose. $$E = H_T \times W_T = 12.0 \times 0.12 = 1.44 \text{ Sv}$$ **Interpretation:** Despite α contributing only 20% of the absorbed dose, it accounts for **83% of the equivalent dose** due to its high LET. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Cumulative Activity & MIRD Dose > **Problem:** A spherical thyroid remnant has uptake of **80 MBq of I-131** immediately post-administration. Effective half-life $T_{eff}$ = **5 days**. The S-value (thyroid self-dose) for I-131 is **2.3 × 10⁻⁴ Gy/(MBq·s)**. Compute the absorbed dose. **Step 1:** Compute decay constant. $$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{5 \times 86400} = 1.604 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2:** Compute cumulative activity. $$\tilde{A} = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1.604 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\tilde{A} = 4.99 \times 10^{13} \text{ Bq·s}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to MBq·s. $$\tilde{A} = \frac{4.99 \times 10^{13}}{10^6} = 4.99 \times 10^7 \text{ MBq·s}$$ **Step 4:** Apply MIRD equation. $$D = \tilde{A} \times S = 4.99 \times 10^7 \times 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$D = 1.148 \times 10^4 \text{ Gy}$$ **Step 5:** Sanity check — this is *very high*. In reality, biological clearance limits uptake; the calculation assumes 100% uptake (worst case). **Realistic answer** for 2% uptake: $$\tilde{A}_{realistic} = 0.02 \times 4.99 \times 10^7 = 9.98 \times 10^5 \text{ MBq·s}$$ $$D_{realistic} = 9.98 \times 10^5 \times 2.3 \times 10^{-4} \approx 230 \text{ Gy}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examinations often test whether students correctly identify **uptake percentage**. Missing this single factor costs 2–3 marks. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Radionuclide | Disease | Best Radionuclide | Carrier | Key Property | |---------|------------------|---------|--------------| | Differentiated thyroid carcinoma | I-131 | NaI (NIS uptake) | β⁻ + γ (imaging) | | Metastatic prostate cancer | Lu-177 (or Ac-225) | PSMA-617 | β⁻ (high TI) | | Neuroendocrine tumours | Lu-177 | DOTATATE | β⁻ (SSTR2) | | Bone metastases | Ra-223 | Dichloride (Ca²⁺ mimic) | α (low marrow dose) | | Hepatocellular carcinoma | Y-90 | Glass microspheres | β⁻ (pure, high energy) | | Non-Hodgkin lymphoma | Y-90 or I-131 | Anti-CD20 antibody | β⁻ (radioimmunotherapy) | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Nuclear Medicine Therapy Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Disease Diagnosis
Imaging and Biomarker Confirmation] --> B[Selection of Targeting Vector
Antibody / Peptide / Nanoparticle] B --> C[Bifunctional Chelator Conjugation
DOTA / DTPA / NOTA] C --> D[Radionuclide Labelling
Lu-177 / Y-90 / Ac-225 / Ra-223] D --> E[Quality Control of Radiopharmaceutical
Radiochemical Purity and Sterility] E --> F[Administration to Patient
IV / Oral / Intraarterial] F --> G[In Vivo Biodistribution
Receptor Binding and Uptake] G --> H[Targeted Radioactive Decay
Alpha or Beta or Auger Emission] H --> I[Local DNA Damage and ROS
Tumour Cell Death] I --> J[Post-Therapy Imaging
SPECT or PET for Dosimetry] J --> K[Response Assessment
Tumour Shrinkage and Toxicity Monitoring] K --> L[Follow-up and Retreatment Planning] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Radiopharmaceutical Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Radiopharmaceutical_Assembly direction LR RV[Targeting Vector
Antibody / Peptide / NP] --> CH[Chelator Linker
DOTA / DTPA] CH --> RN[Therapeutic Radionuclide
Lu-177 / Y-90 / Ac-225] RN --> EM[Emission
Beta or Alpha or Auger] end EM --> TM[Tumour Cell DNA Damage] EM --> NM[Normal Tissue Background Dose] TM --> Outcome[Therapeutic Effect] NM --> Tox[Manage Toxicity via TI Optimization] ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — α vs β⁻ Therapy Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Disease Characterization] --> Q1{Tumour Size and Type} Q1 -->|Micrometastases / Single Cells| Alpha[Choose Alpha Emitter
Ra-223 / Ac-225 / Bi-213] Q1 -->|Bulk Solid Tumour / Heterogeneous| Beta[Choose Beta Minus Emitter
Lu-177 / Y-90 / I-131] Alpha --> AlphaCheck{High LET
Short Range
DNA DSB} Beta --> BetaCheck{Low LET
Longer Range
Crossfire Effect} AlphaCheck --> Out1[Best for Circulating Tumour Cells
and Minimal Disease] BetaCheck --> Out2[Best for Established Solid Masses
and Cross-dose Effect] ``` ## 4.4 Nano-Bioengineering Convergence Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Nano[Nanocarrier Design
Liposomes / Polymeric NPs / Gold NPs / Dendrimers] --> Load[Radionuclide Loading
Lu-177 / Re-188 / Ac-225] Load --> Targ[Active Targeting
RGD peptide / Folate / Antibody fragment] Targ --> EPR[EPR Effect Enhancement
Passive Tumour Accumulation] EPR --> Trigger[pH or Enzyme Triggered Release
Site Specific Uncapping] Trigger --> Kill[Localized Cytotoxic Radiation
Therapy plus Imaging Theranostics] ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams follow the **block-level functional architecture** convention recommended for KTU answer scripts where physical drawings are not feasible. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Targeted Radionuclide Therapy (TRT). List the three essential components of a therapeutic radiopharmaceutical.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Targeted Radionuclide Therapy is a clinical modality that uses **unsealed radioactive sources** to deliver cytotoxic ionizing radiation **selectively to diseased cells** via biologically targeted molecules. The three essential components of a therapeutic radiopharmaceutical are: 1. **Targeting vector** — antibody, peptide, small molecule, or nanoparticle that confers biological specificity. 2. **Radionuclide** — radioactive atom (e.g., Lu-177, Y-90, Ac-225) that emits therapeutic ionizing radiation (α, β⁻, or Auger electrons). 3. **Bifunctional chelator** — chemical linker (e.g., DOTA, DTPA) that binds the radionuclide firmly to the targeting vector without losing biological activity. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is the biological half-life of the carrier molecule matched with the physical half-life of the radionuclide? Give one example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Matching the biological half-life of the carrier with the physical half-life of the radionuclide is critical because: 1. If **T_phys ≫ T_bio**, the radionuclide decays *after* the carrier is cleared from the body → most of the radioactive decay is wasted, reducing tumour dose. 2. If **T_phys ≪ T_bio**, decay is complete before the carrier reaches and binds to the tumour → no therapeutic effect. **Example:** Lu-177 (T₁/₂ = 6.64 d) is well-matched with DOTATATE peptide (T_bio ≈ hours–days) for neuroendocrine tumours, giving an effective half-life of 3–5 days. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Describe the MIRD (Medical Internal Radiation Dose) formalism for calculating the absorbed dose to a target organ from an internally administered radiopharmaceutical. Define cumulative activity and the S-value.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A patient is administered 185 MBq of I-131 for thyroid remnant ablation. The thyroid uptake is 5% at 24 hours, and the effective half-life in the thyroid is 6 days. Calculate:** 1. **The cumulative activity in the thyroid.** 2. **The absorbed dose if the S-value (thyroid self-dose for I-131) is 1.4 × 10⁻⁴ Gy/(MBq·s).** --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks The **MIRD formalism** calculates the mean absorbed dose to a target tissue (T) from a source tissue (S): **Step 1:** Cumulative activity in the source organ. $$\tilde{A}_S = \int_0^\infty A_S(t)\, dt$$ For a single-exponential clearance: $$\tilde{A}_S = \frac{A_0}{\lambda_{eff}} = A_0 \times \tau_{eff} = \frac{A_0 \times T_{eff}}{\ln 2}$$ **Step 2:** The **S-value** $S(T \leftarrow S)$ is the mean absorbed dose to target T per unit cumulative activity in source S. It depends on: - Radionuclide emission spectrum (energies and yields of all emissions) - Source organ mass - Target organ mass - Cross-organ geometry (self-dose vs. cross-dose) **Step 3:** MIRD absorbed dose equation. $$\boxed{D(T \leftarrow S) = \tilde{A}_S \times S(T \leftarrow S)}$$ **Step 4:** For multiple source organs, sum all contributions: $$D_T = \sum_S \tilde{A}_S \times S(T \leftarrow S)$$ The S-value encapsulates the absorbed fraction $\phi_i$ of each emission and the target organ mass: $$S(T \leftarrow S) = \frac{1}{m_T} \sum_i Y_i \, E_i \, \phi_i(T \leftarrow S)$$ where $Y_i$ is the yield, $E_i$ is the energy, and $\phi_i$ is the absorbed fraction of emission *i*. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $$A_{admin} = 185 \text{ MBq}, \quad \text{Uptake} = 5\%, \quad T_{eff} = 6 \text{ days}, \quad S = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Gy/(MBq·s)}$$ **Step 1:** Calculate activity in thyroid immediately post-uptake (at 24 h). $$A_0 = 0.05 \times 185 = 9.25 \text{ MBq}$$ **Step 2:** Compute effective decay constant. $$\lambda_{eff} = \frac{0.693}{6 \times 86400} = \frac{0.693}{518400} = 1.337 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3:** Compute cumulative activity. $$\tilde{A} = \frac{A_0}{\lambda_{eff}} = \frac{9.25 \times 10^6 \text{ Bq}}{1.337 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ $$\tilde{A} = 6.92 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq·s}$$ Converting to MBq·s: $$\tilde{A} = 6.92 \times 10^6 \text{ MBq·s}$$ **Step 4:** Apply MIRD equation. $$D = \tilde{A} \times S = 6.92 \times 10^6 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{D = 968.8 \text{ Gy}}$$ This very high dose is *intentional* — thyroid remnant ablation requires **>300 Gy** to fully destroy residual tissue. --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Compare α-emitting and β⁻-emitting radionuclides for cancer therapy. Include in your answer: LET, tissue range, RBE, suitable disease targets, and one example for each.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain how nanoparticles (liposomes / polymeric NPs / gold NPs) are being used in modern nuclear medicine therapy to improve the therapeutic index. Discuss the EPR effect.** --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks | Parameter | β⁻ Emitters | α Emitters | |-----------|-------------|------------| | **Examples** | I-131, Lu-177, Y-90 | Ra-223, Ac-225, Bi-213 | | **LET (keV/μm)** | 0.2 – 1.0 (low) | 50 – 230 (high) | | **Tissue range** | 0.5 – 12 mm | 40 – 100 μm | | **Tracks per cell** | Hundreds – thousands | 1 – 20 | | **DNA damage type** | Single / double strand breaks | Complex clustered DSBs | | **RBE (relative biological effectiveness)** | ~1 | 5 – 20 | | **Oxygen dependence** | Less oxygen-dependent | Largely independent (kills hypoxic cells) | | **Best disease target** | Bulk tumours, heterogeneous masses | Micrometastases, single cells, leukaemia | | **Major toxicity** | Bone marrow (wider range) | Kidney (proximal tubules) | **Conclusion:** α-emitters are preferred for **minimal residual disease and micrometastases**; β⁻ emitters are preferred for **established bulky tumours**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Nano-engineered carriers** enhance Nuclear Medicine Therapy through: 1. **Multivalent Loading:** A single 100 nm liposome can carry **10³ – 10⁴ radionuclide atoms**, dramatically increasing local dose per binding event. 2. **Passive Targeting via EPR Effect:** Tumour vasculature is *leaky* (gaps of 200 nm – 2 μm between endothelial cells) and lymphatic drainage is impaired. Nanoparticles in the **10 – 200 nm size range** preferentially accumulate in tumour interstitium — termed the **Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect**. 3. **Active Targeting:** Surface functionalization with RGD peptide (binds αvβ3 integrin), folic acid, or antibody fragments (e.g., anti-EGFR) increases tumour cell uptake 5–10×. 4. **Triggered Release:** pH-sensitive (tumour pH ≈ 6.5) or enzyme-cleavable (MMP-2/9) linkers release radionuclide payload *intratumourally*, sparing healthy tissue. 5. **Theranostics:** Same NP loaded with therapeutic (e.g., Lu-177) and imaging (e.g., Ga-68 or Cu-64) radionuclides enables **real-time dosimetry**. **Example:** PEGylated liposomes loaded with Re-188 (β⁻) — under clinical evaluation for hepatocellular carcinoma. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Forgetting the uptake fraction:** If a problem states 5% thyroid uptake, students often ignore it. Always multiply by uptake. **Penalty: –2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing physical vs. effective half-life:** Use $T_{eff}$ (not $T_{1/2,phys}$) when biological clearance is given. **Penalty: –3 marks.** > 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing MBq with s⁻¹ in the same equation without converting to Bq. **Penalty: –1 to –2 marks.** > 4. **Skipping the integral in cumulative activity:** Students often write $\tilde{A} = A_0 \cdot T_{eff}$ without justification. Always show the derivation: $\tilde{A} = \int A_0 e^{-\lambda t} dt = A_0/\lambda$. > 5. **Writing "Gy" instead of "Sv" for weighted doses:** Equivalent and effective doses use Sieverts (Sv). Absorbed dose uses Gray (Gy). > 6. **Treating S-value as universal:** S-value depends on *both* source and target organ — never quote it without specifying both. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **NMT** = use of unsealed radionuclides to deliver cytotoxic radiation **selectively**. - **Three pillars of a radiopharmaceutical:** Targeting Vector + Bifunctional Chelator + Radionuclide. - **Decay Law:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. - **Decay constant:** $\lambda = 0.693/T_{1/2}$. - **Cumulative Activity (MIRD):** $\tilde{A} = A_0/\lambda = 1.443 \times A_0 \times T_{1/2}$. - **Absorbed Dose (MIRD):** $D = \tilde{A} \times S(T \leftarrow S)$. - **Effective Half-life:** $1/T_{eff} = 1/T_{bio} + 1/T_{phys}$. - **Equivalent Dose:** $H = D \times W_R$; **Effective Dose:** $E = \sum H_T \times W_T$. - **$W_R$ (β⁻) = 1**; **$W_R$ (α) = 20**. - **α-emitters** → high LET (50–230 keV/μm), short range (40–100 μm), high RBE → **micrometastases**. - **β⁻-emitters** → low LET, range 0.5–12 mm → **bulk tumours**; "crossfire effect" helps heterogeneous tumours. - **Auger emitters** → sub-cellular range (nm) → require **nuclear/internalized** targeting. - **Key clinical examples:** I-131 (thyroid), Lu-177-DOTATATE (NET), Lu-177-PSMA (prostate), Y-90 (liver), Ra-223 (bone mets). - **Nano-carrier advantages:** Multivalent loading, EPR effect, active targeting, triggered release, theranostics. - **EPR Effect:** passive tumour accumulation of 10–200 nm NPs due to leaky vasculature and impaired lymphatic drainage. - **Therapeutic Index:** $TI = D_{tumour}/D_{normal\ organ}$ — maximise it! - **Units:** 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq; 1 Gy = 1 J/kg; 1 Sv = 1 J/kg (weighted). - **Standard chelators:** DOTA (for Lu, Y, Ac), DTPA (for In, Y), NOTA (for Ga, Cu). - **Always** show the integral in cumulative activity derivations. - **Always** quote the uptake percentage in clinical dosimetry problems. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic Notes — Nuclear Medicine Therapy.** This document aligns with **KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBBT632, Module 3**. For Module 3's other sub-topics (Imaging Agents, Radiopharmaceutical Design), follow the same analytical framework.

topics.title: Photodynamic Therapy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT632 - NANO BIOENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photodynamic Therapy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photodynamic Therapy (PDT)** is a clinically approved, minimally invasive therapeutic modality that utilizes the **photochemical interaction** between three essential components—a **photosensitizing agent (PS)**, a **specific wavelength of light**, and **molecular oxygen (³O₂)**—to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) that selectively destroy targeted abnormal cells, tissues, or microbial pathogens. The term "Photodynamic" originates from the Greek roots *photo* (light) and *dynamic* (action/change), signifying a light-activated chemical transformation distinct from photothermal (heat-based) or photomechanical (shockwave-based) therapies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PDT is studied under **Module 3 (Nano-Enabled Therapeutics & Diagnostics)** because modern third-generation photosensitizers are increasingly engineered using **nanoparticles (NPs)** — such as liposomes, polymeric NPs, quantum dots, gold nanorods, and mesoporous silica — to overcome limitations of conventional PS molecules.

In the context of **Nano Bioengineering**, PDT is an exemplary case of *theranostics* (therapy + diagnostics), where nanostructured photosensitizers enable simultaneous imaging and treatment of malignancies.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of PDT (Jablonski Diagram Triad)

PDT requires the synchronous convergence of three independently insufficient components. Removing any one completely abolishes the cytotoxic effect.

| # | Component | Role in PDT | Typical Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Photosensitizer (PS)** | Light-absorbing molecule that undergoes excitation and energy transfer | Photofrin®, 5-ALA, Chlorin e6, Ce6-loaded PLGA NPs |
| 2 | **Light Source** | Provides photons of specific wavelength matching the PS absorption peak | He-Ne laser (632.8 nm), diode laser (660 nm), LED arrays |
| 3 | **Molecular Oxygen (³O₂)** | Accepts energy from excited PS to form cytotoxic singlet oxygen (¹O₂) | Tissue-resident ground-state oxygen |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition Box:** *“Photodynamic therapy is a form of phototherapy involving light and a photosensitizing chemical substance used in conjunction with molecular oxygen to elicit cell death (phototoxicity).”* — Adapted for KTU 2024 Module 3 description.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍎

Imagine you are holding a **light-sensitive firecracker** in a dark room:

- **The firecracker = Photosensitizer (PS):** It is harmless by itself. It is sitting quietly, chemically inert, doing nothing.
- **The flashlight (matching the exact color needed) = Light of specific wavelength:** Without the right *color* (wavelength) of light, even shining a very bright white light will not ignite the firecracker. Each photosensitizer has a unique "ignition wavelength" (absorption maximum, λ_max).
- **The air in the room = Molecular Oxygen (O₂):** Even with the firecracker and the right light, without oxygen, the firecracker will not explode. The reaction *needs* oxygen to produce the destructive energy.
- **The EXPLOSION = Singlet Oxygen (¹O₂) + ROS:** The synchronized combination of all three produces a localized "explosion" of reactive oxygen species that destroys the targeted cell — but leaves surrounding healthy cells largely untouched, because the photosensitizer preferentially accumulates in diseased tissue.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students — "PS-L-O"**: **P**hotosensitizer, **S**pecific **L**ight, **O**xygen. Memorize the triplet; it is the most frequently asked short-answer question in KTU exams.

## 1.4 Brief Historical Evolution 📜

| Era | Photosensitizer Generation | Key Milestone |
|-----|----------------------------|----------------|
| 1900s | — | Oscar Raab (Munich) observed paramecia death under light + acridine |
| 1970s–80s | **First Generation** | Hematoporphyrin derivative (HpD) / Photofrin® (FDA approval 1995 for bladder cancer) |
| 1990s–2000s | **Second Generation** | 5-ALA, Chlorin e6, mTHPC (Foscan®), phthalocyanines |
| 2010s–Present | **Third Generation** | **Nanoparticle-conjugated PS** — liposomal, polymeric, metallic NP systems |

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants/metrics are critical for numerical problems in KTU exams:
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$
> - **Photon energy formula:** $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$
> - **Standard PDT treatment wavelength (red light):** $\lambda \approx 630\text{–}700 \, \text{nm}$ (penetration depth ~ 5–10 mm)
> - **Singlet oxygen quantum yield (Φ_Δ)** of an ideal PS ≈ **0.5–1.0** (unitless)
> - **Typical PS dose:** 2–5 mg/kg body weight
> - **Typical light fluence:** 50–150 J/cm²
> - **Typical irradiance:** 100 mW/cm²

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Mechanism — The Jablonski Diagram (Step-by-Step)

The photochemistry of PDT is best understood through the **Jablonski Diagram**, which maps the electronic energy states and transitions of the photosensitizer molecule.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Ground State (S₀):** The photosensitizer molecule exists in its lowest, stable electronic energy state (singlet ground state). All electrons are paired with opposite spins.

2. **Photon Absorption (S₀ → S₁):** The PS absorbs a photon of energy $E = h\nu$ that matches the energy gap $\Delta E$ between HOMO and LUMO orbitals.
   - The molecule is promoted to the **first excited singlet state (S₁)** — a higher-energy electronic state where one electron is unpaired but still in opposite spin to its partner.

3. **Internal Conversion (S₁ → S₁):** The excited PS undergoes vibrational relaxation, losing energy as heat to the surrounding solvent/tissue environment.

4. **Fluorescence (S₁ → S₀):** A portion of the energy is released as fluorescence emission. This is the principle behind **PS fluorescence imaging/diagnostics** — a key theranostic feature.

5. **Intersystem Crossing (ISC) (S₁ → T₁):** A critical spin-forbidden but spin-orbit-coupling-enabled transition. The electron *flips* its spin, producing the **first excited triplet state (T₁)**. T₁ is longer-lived (microseconds to milliseconds vs. nanoseconds for S₁) and is responsible for the photodynamic reactions.

6. **Type I Reaction (T₁ + substrate):** The triplet PS directly transfers an electron (or hydrogen atom) to biological substrates (lipids, proteins, DNA), generating radical species:
   - Superoxide anion $\text{O}_2^{\bullet -}$
   - Hydroxyl radical $\text{OH}^{\bullet}$
   - Hydrogen peroxide $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$

7. **Type II Reaction (T₁ + ³O₂):** The triplet PS transfers its excitation energy directly to ground-state molecular oxygen (³O₂), producing **singlet oxygen (¹O₂)**, the principal cytotoxic agent in PDT. ¹O₂ is highly electrophilic and has a lifetime of ~ 10–320 ns in biological media, diffusing ~ 10–55 nm before reacting.

8. **Biomolecular Damage:** ROS/¹O₂ oxidize critical cellular components → cell death via apoptosis, necrosis, or autophagy.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Exam Insight:** The KTU board examiners *frequently* ask students to "Differentiate Type I and Type II photodynamic reactions." Always state which ROS is produced by each, and the spin state of the oxygen involved.

## 2.2 The Three Cell Death Pathways Triggered by PDT

| Pathway | Characteristics | When Prevalent |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Apoptosis** | Programmed, energy-dependent cell death; non-inflammatory | Low PS dose, low light fluence |
| **Necrosis** | Uncontrolled rupture, inflammatory | High PS dose, high light fluence |
| **Autophagy** | Self-digestion via lysosomes | Moderate oxidative stress |

## 2.3 Nano-Photosensitizers — Why Nanotechnology? 🧬

Conventional first-/second-generation PS suffer from:
- **Poor aqueous solubility** (porphyrins are hydrophobic)
- **Lack of selectivity** for tumor tissue
- **Prolonged skin photosensitivity** (especially Photofrin®, lasting 4–6 weeks)
- **Self-aggregation** in aqueous media → reduced ¹O₂ yield
- **Rapid clearance** from the body

**Nanotechnology offers solutions via:**

| Nano-Platform | Advantage in PDT |
|---------------|------------------|
| **Liposomes** | Encapsulate hydrophobic PS; improve biocompatibility |
| **Polymeric NPs (PLGA, PEG)** | Controlled/sustained PS release; EPR effect for tumor targeting |
| **Gold Nanorods / Nanostars** | Combine photothermal (PTT) + PDT in single agent |
| **Quantum Dots (CdSe/ZnS)** | Tuneable emission + energy transfer to PS |
| **Mesoporous Silica NPs (MSN)** | High PS loading capacity in pores |
| **Upconversion Nanoparticles (UCNPs)** | Convert NIR (980 nm) → visible light for deeper tissue PDT |
| **Carbon Dots / Graphene Oxide** | Excellent PS carriers; intrinsic fluorescence imaging |
| **Nanoemulsions** | Improved topical PS delivery for skin PDT |

> [!NOTE]
> **EPR Effect (Enhanced Permeability and Retention):** Tumor vasculature is "leaky" with poor lymphatic drainage. NPs of 10–200 nm preferentially accumulate in tumor interstitium, providing **passive targeting** — a cornerstone of nano-PDT.

## 2.4 Light Sources in Clinical PDT

| Light Source | Wavelength | Application |
|--------------|------------|-------------|
| Argon-pumped dye laser | 630 nm | Historical gold standard |
| He-Ne laser | 632.8 nm | Dermatology |
| Diode laser | 660–690 nm | Modern clinical PDT |
| LED arrays | 400–700 nm | Cost-effective, field-treatment |
| Optical fiber delivery | Various | Interstitial PDT (deep tumors) |
| UCNPs + NIR | 980 nm | **Deep-tissue nano-PDT** (research frontier) |

The **"Optical Window of Tissue"** (650–900 nm) is critical — light in this range penetrates tissue with minimum absorption by hemoglobin (Hb) and water.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (never bare `|`) inside table cells to avoid Markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula / Constant | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu$ | Energy of one photon | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy in terms of wavelength | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$ ; $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Frequency from wavelength | Hz |
| 4 | $\Phi_{\Delta} = \dfrac{\text{number of }\,^1\text{O}_2 \text{ molecules}}{\text{number of photons absorbed}}$ | Singlet oxygen quantum yield | 0 to 1 (ideal ≈ 0.5–1.0) |
| 5 | $^1\text{O}_2 \text{ quantum yield} = \Phi_{\text{ISC}} \times \Phi_{\text{energy transfer}}$ | Overall ¹O₂ yield (Type II) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Light Fluence} = \text{Irradiance} \times \text{Time}$ | Total light dose | $\text{J/cm}^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{Beer-Lambert Law}: A = \varepsilon c \ell$ | Light attenuation in tissue | $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹) |
| 8 | $\text{Tissue Penetration Depth } d \approx \dfrac{1}{\mu_a + \mu_s}$ | Where $\mu_a, \mu_s$ are absorption & scattering coefficients | mm (≈ 5–10 mm at 630 nm) |
| 9 | $\text{PS Dose} = 2\text{–}5 \, \text{mg/kg}$ | Standard clinical dose | mg per kg body weight |
| 10 | $\text{Drug-to-Light Interval (DLI)} = 3\text{–}96 \, \text{h}$ | Time between PS administration and illumination | hours |
| 11 | $\text{Singlet oxygen lifetime} \tau_{^1\text{O}_2} \approx 10\text{–}320 \, \text{ns}$ | In biological media | nanoseconds |
| 12 | $\text{Diffusion length} L = \sqrt{6 D \tau}$ | Distance ¹O₂ diffuses | ~ 10–55 nm in cells |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oncology:** Treatment of basal cell carcinoma, head & neck cancer, esophageal cancer, lung cancer.
- **Dermatology:** Actinic keratosis, acne, psoriasis.
- **Ophthalmology:** Age-related macular degeneration (PDT with Verteporfin).
- **Antimicrobial PDT (aPDT):** Eradication of drug-resistant bacteria (MRSA), fungi, viruses — a major field.
- **Dental PDT:** Periodontal pathogens, root canal disinfection.
- **Veterinary medicine:** Equine and canine cancer treatment.
- **Cardiovascular PDT:** Prevention of restenosis after angioplasty (PhotoPoint® system).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Photon Energy Corresponding to PDT Treatment Wavelength

### Problem Statement
A diode laser used in clinical photodynamic therapy emits red light of wavelength $\lambda = 660 \, \text{nm}$. Calculate the energy of a single photon emitted by this laser. Also, calculate the number of photons delivered per second when the laser power output is 100 mW. (This is a standard KTU-style numerical problem.)

### Given Data
- Wavelength $\lambda = 660 \, \text{nm} = 660 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$
- Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$
- Laser power $P = 100 \, \text{mW} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} = 0.1 \, \text{J/s}$

### Step 1 — Write down the governing formula
The energy of a single photon is given by the Planck–Einstein relation:

$$E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the constants and the wavelength
$$E = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}) \times (3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s})}{660 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator calculation
$$h \times c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} \, \text{J \cdot m}$$

### Step 4 — Division by wavelength
$$E = \dfrac{19.878 \times 10^{-26}}{660 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{19.878 \times 10^{-26}}{6.60 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$E = 3.012 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to electron-volts (eV) for biological relevance
$$1 \, \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$$

$$E = \dfrac{3.012 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.88 \, \text{eV}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate number of photons per second (photon flux)
Energy per photon = $3.012 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$. With laser power = 0.1 J/s:

$$N = \dfrac{P}{E_{\text{photon}}} = \dfrac{0.1 \, \text{J/s}}{3.012 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}}$$

$$N = 3.32 \times 10^{17} \, \text{photons/s}$$

### Step 7 — Engineering interpretation
- Each photon carries $\approx 3 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$ (equivalent to 1.88 eV).
- A modest 100 mW laser delivers $\sim 3.3 \times 10^{17}$ photons per second.
- This enormous photon flux ensures ample activation of the PS in the target tissue.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** This derivation appears in almost every KTU question paper in modified form. Always show all 7 steps — do NOT skip the unit conversions, as examiners award fractional marks for each correct step.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Optical Penetration Depth of Tissue

### Problem Statement
The absorption coefficient of human soft tissue at $\lambda = 630 \, \text{nm}$ is $\mu_a = 0.5 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$ and the reduced scattering coefficient is $\mu_s' = 12 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$. Estimate the effective attenuation coefficient and the optical penetration depth.

### Governing Equation (Diffusion Approximation)
$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{3 \mu_a (\mu_a + \mu_s')}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute values
$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.5 \times (0.5 + 12)}$$

### Step 2 — Add the term inside parentheses
$$(\mu_a + \mu_s') = 0.5 + 12 = 12.5 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply
$$3 \times 0.5 \times 12.5 = 1.5 \times 12.5 = 18.75 \, \text{cm}^{-2}$$

### Step 4 — Take the square root
$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{18.75} = 4.33 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

### Step 5 — Penetration depth (where fluence drops to $1/e$ of surface value)
$$d = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{1}{4.33 \, \text{cm}^{-1}} = 0.231 \, \text{cm} = 2.31 \, \text{mm}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation
- 630 nm light penetrates only ~ 2–3 mm into soft tissue, limiting superficial PDT.
