KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. Define a biomedical shunt. Differentiate between VP and VA shunts. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT632]` **Model Answer:** A biomedical shunt is an implantable device that diverts excess physiological fluid from one anatomical site to another to relieve pathological pressure. A **ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt** drains CSF from the lateral ventricle into the peritoneal cavity, while a **ventriculoatrial (VA) shunt** drains CSF into the right atrium of the heart. VP shunts are preferred due to lower risk of septic emboli and easier surgical revision, whereas VA shunts are reserved for patients with abdominal contraindications (e.g., peritonitis, prior abdominal surgery). **[3 marks]** #### Q2. List any four failure modes of conventional CSF shunts and identify a nano-engineering solution for each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBBT632]` **Model Answer:** | Failure Mode | Nano-engineering Solution | |---|---| | 1. Proximal catheter clogging by choroid plexus | Nanopatterned catheter inlet holes; lotus-effect surfaces | | 2. Valve biofouling by protein adsorption | Zwitterionic polymer brush coatings | | 3. Bacterial infection and biofilm | Silver nanoparticle coatings | | 4. Mechanical wear of valve seat | TiO₂ or graphene oxide nanocomposite seats | **[3 marks: 0.5 each for failure, 0.25 each for solution]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEBBT632]** **Q (a)** Explain the anatomy and working of a **programmable differential-pressure valve** used in CSF shunting. Discuss the role of **anti-siphon devices**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A programmable differential-pressure (DP) valve is the most clinically sophisticated shunt valve. It consists of a **titanium or ruby ball-and-spring assembly** whose spring tension is adjusted externally using a magnetic rotor. The clinician places a programming tool over the valve (behind the ear) and rotates a stepper mechanism to set the opening pressure, typically between **30 and 200 mm H₂O** in 5–10 mm increments. **Working principle:** - When **ICP > set pressure**, the ball is pushed off its seat and CSF flows through the valve. - When **ICP < set pressure**, the spring keeps the ball closed, preventing overdrainage. - A **pressure-flow curve** is non-linear; flow depends on the square root of ΔP above threshold. **Anti-siphon devices:** - When a patient stands up, the hydrostatic column of CSF in the distal catheter creates a **siphoning effect**, often causing **overdrainage** and complications such as subdural hematoma. - The **anti-siphon device (ASD)** is a membrane that **collapses** under negative pressure in the upright position, breaking the siphon and protecting the brain. - The Orbis-Sigma Delta valve integrates this with a **flow-regulating stage** that maintains constant flow over a wide ΔP range. **[7 marks: 2 definition + 2 working + 2 anti-siphon + 1 clinical relevance]** **Q (b)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar Newtonian flow through a cylindrical catheter. A shunt catheter of length 12 cm and inner diameter 1.0 mm drains CSF (viscosity $0.7 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) with a flow of 0.4 mL/min. Compute the pressure drop and verify that the flow is laminar. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** (See Section 3.1 derivation for full flow.) **Step 1 — Hagen-Poiseuille result:** $$ Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L} $$ **Step 2 — Given data:** - $L = 0.12$ m, $d = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $\mu = 0.7 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $Q = 0.4$ mL/min $= 6.667 \times 10^{-9}$ m³/s **Step 3 — Compute pressure drop:** $$ \Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^4} $$ $$ r^4 = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ 8\mu L Q = 8 \times 0.7 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-9} = 4.48 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ \pi r^4 = 3.1416 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} = 1.963 \times 10^{-13} $$ $$ \Delta P = \frac{4.48 \times 10^{-12}}{1.963 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 22.82 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Velocity and Reynolds number:** $$ v = \frac{Q}{\pi r^2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-9}}{7.854 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.49 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} $$ $$ Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 8.49 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.7 \times 10^{-3}} = 12.13 $$ Since **Re = 12.13 ≪ 2000**, flow is **laminar** ✓ — Poiseuille's law is valid. **[7 marks: 2 derivation outline + 3 numerical + 2 verification]** --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEBBT632]** **Q (a)** With neat schematics, classify **biomedical shunts** based on their terminal drainage site. Discuss the components of a standard shunt system. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** (Refer to Section 2.1 and 2.2 for detailed classification.) **Classification by distal site:** 1. **Ventriculoperitoneal (VP)** — drains into peritoneum (most common). 2. **Ventriculoatrial (VA)** — drains into right atrium. 3. **Lumboperitoneal (LP)** — from lumbar thecal sac to peritoneum. 4. **Ventriculopleural (VPL)** — drains into pleural cavity. **Standard shunt components:** 1. **Proximal catheter** with multiple perforations for CSF intake. 2. **Valve** for pressure/flow regulation. 3. **Reservoir/dome** for sampling and palpation-based pressure testing. 4. **Distal catheter** that tunnels subcutaneously to the absorption site. 5. **Connectors** to join the segments. **[7 marks: 3 for classification + 4 for components with role of each]** **Q (b)** Discuss the **failure mechanisms** of conventional shunts and explain how **nanotechnology-based interventions** can address each. Evaluate the impact on shunt longevity and patient outcome. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Failure mechanisms and nano-solutions:** | Failure | Mechanism | Nano-solution | |---|---|---| | 1. **Proximal obstruction** | Choroid plexus ingrowth, cellular debris | Nanopatterned inlet (micro/nano pillars) preventing cellular adhesion | | 2. **Valve biofouling** | Protein adsorption → fibrous overgrowth | Zwitterionic polymer brushes (polySBMA) forming hydration layer | | 3. **Bacterial infection** | *Staphylococcus epidermidis* biofilm formation | Silver nanoparticles (Ag-NP) or copper nanoparticle coatings providing bacteriostatic action | | 4. **Mechanical wear** | Silicone fatigue, valve seat erosion | Graphene oxide reinforced silicone; TiO₂ nanocomposite valve seats | | 5. **Overdrainage / underdrainage** | Lack of dynamic feedback | NEMS-based wireless ICP sensors integrated into the valve | **Impact:** - Pre-clinical studies show **50–80% reduction in bacterial colonization** with Ag-NP coatings. - **Zwitterionic coatings** reduce protein adsorption by >90% compared to bare silicone. - Combined nano-engineered shunts have demonstrated **>2× improvement in mean patency** in animal trials. **[7 marks: 3 failure mechanisms + 3 nano-solutions + 1 outcome evaluation]** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss Areas in "Shunts" Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting units** — Always state Pa vs. mm H₂O explicitly. 1 mm H₂O ≈ 9.81 Pa. > 2. **Confusing ΔP with absolute ICP** — The valve opens on ΔP, not absolute ICP. > 3. **Using the wrong diameter** — Always use **inner diameter** in Poiseuille's law; KTU answer keys are strict on this. > 4. **Skipping Reynolds number check** — Even if not asked, including it earns an extra mark. > 5. **Misidentifying the failure mode** — Proximal catheter obstruction is due to **choroid plexus**, not "debris" alone. Be specific. > 6. **Forgetting anti-siphon role** — Overdrainage is a recognized complication; examiners expect anti-siphon discussion. > 7. **Stating "nano" without specifying which nanomaterial** — Always name the nanomaterial (Ag, TiO₂, GO, zwitterionic brush) and explain its action. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shunts (PEBBT632, Module 4):** - **Definition:** A shunt is an implantable conduit that diverts biological fluid (CSF, blood) to relieve abnormal pressure. - **Primary clinical indication:** **Hydrocephalus** — both communicating and non-communicating. - **Normal CSF:** Produced at **~0.3 mL/min**, total volume ~150 mL, recycled 3–4×/day. - **Normal ICP:** **5–15 cm H₂O** (~0.5–1.5 kPa). - **Most common shunt type:** **Ventriculoperitoneal (VP)**. - **Components:** Proximal catheter + Valve + Distal catheter (+ optional reservoir). - **Valve types:** Differential pressure (fixed/programmable), flow-regulated, anti-siphon. - **Master equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ — note the **r⁴ dependence**. - **Laminar regime:** Re ≪ 2000 (typically Re ~ 10–20 for CSF shunts). - **Hydrodynamic resistance analog:** $R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ — series additivity. - **Failure rate of conventional shunts:** ~**40–50% within 2 years**. - **Nano-engineering interventions:** - **Nanopatterned surfaces** → anti-adhesion - **Silver nanoparticles** → antibacterial - **Zwitterionic brushes** → ultralow protein fouling - **Graphene oxide / TiO₂ nanocomposites** → mechanical durability - **NEMS sensors** → wireless ICP feedback - **Design insight:** The catheter's pressure drop is **far smaller than the valve's**; therefore the **valve is the rate-limiting element**, not the catheter. - **Siphoning effect:** In upright posture, hydrostatic column can cause **overdrainage** → anti-siphon devices are essential. - **Biotribology:** Wall shear stress ≈ **20–30 mPa** in normal CSF flow; insufficient to detach mature biofilms, motivating nano-engineering. **End of Shunts — Module 4 Notes**
